perturbation.original_text: Late payments shall accrue interest at a rate equal to fifteen (15%) percent per annum.
perturbation.changed_text: Late payments shall accrue interest at a rate equal to twenty-six (26%) percent per annum.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the late payment interest rate to 26% per annum may violate state usury laws. Usury laws set a maximum interest rate that can be charged on loans and debts.
perturbation.law_explanation: New York law sets the legal rate of interest at 16% per annum. Charging 26% would be usurious under NY law. The contract specifies New York law applies in Section 10(b).

See most recent version before or on: 2014-09-22 2015-10-09 2017-10-27 2018-04-20 2018-07-06 2018-07-13 2018-08-31 2019-01-11 2019-01-18 2019-06-28 2019-07-05 2019-07-19 2019-08-16 2019-10-18 2019-11-22 2019-12-13 2020-02-21 2020-04-10 2020-12-18 2021-01-08 2021-04-02 2021-04-09 2021-06-18 2021-09-24 2021-12-31 2022-02-25 2022-03-04 2022-12-30 2023-01-06 2023-10-27 2023-11-26 2024-12-20 2025-01-03 Share Facebook Email SECTION 5-501 Rate of interest; usury forbidden General Obligations (GOB) CHAPTER 24-A, ARTICLE 5, TITLE 5 § 5-501. Rate of interest; usury forbidden. 1. The rate of interest, as computed pursuant to this title, upon the loan or forbearance of any money, goods, or things in action, except as provided in subdivisions five and six of this section or as otherwise provided by law, shall be six per centum per annum unless a different rate is prescribed in section fourteen-a of the banking law. 2. No person or corporation shall, directly or indirectly, charge, take or receive any money, goods or things in action as interest on the loan or forbearance of any money, goods or things in action at a rate exceeding the rate above prescribed. The amount charged, taken or received as interest shall include any and all amounts paid or payable, directly or indirectly, by any person, to or for the account of the lender in consideration for making the loan or forbearance as defined by the superintendent of financial services pursuant to subdivision three of section fourteen-a of the banking law except such fee as may be fixed by the commissioner of taxation and finance as the cost of servicing loans made by the property and liability insurance security fund. 3. If the rate of interest charged, taken or received on any loan or forbearance secured primarily by either (i) an interest in real property improved by a one to six family residence occupied by the owner or (ii) certificates of stock or other evidence of an ownership interest in a corporation or partnership formed for the purpose of the cooperative ownership of real estate taken as security for a loan under subdivision five of section one hundred three of the banking law, subdivision eight-a of section two hundred thirty-five of such law or subdivision two-a of section three hundred eighty of such law, exceeds six per centum per annum, a. in the case of a loan referred to by clause (i) of this subdivision, the term of such loan or forbearan
perturbation.justification: The changed text specifies an interest rate of 26% per annum for late payments, while the original text specified 15%. The Law Explanation says this violates New York law, which sets the legal rate of interest at 16% per annum. Scraped Law Snippet 1 states in CHAPTER 24-A, ARTICLE 5, TITLE 5 § 5-501 that the rate of interest shall be six per centum per annum unless otherwise provided, and that no person or corporation shall charge a rate exceeding the rate prescribed. The change to 26% directly contradicts this law, as it exceeds the legally permissible interest rate.

perturbation.original_text: (c) Tadeo shall have the right (but not the obligation) to terminate this Agreement and the rights granted to Diplomat hereunder if: (i) Diplomat is in material breach of any of its obligations hereunder, which breach is not cured within five days of receipt of written notice from Tadeo of such breach;
perturbation.changed_text: (c) Tadeo shall have the right (but not the obligation) to terminate this Agreement and the rights granted to Diplomat hereunder if: (i) Diplomat is in material breach of any of its obligations hereunder, which breach is not cured within one day of receipt of written notice from Tadeo of such breach;
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the cure period for a material breach to one day may be considered unconscionable and violate the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing, depending on the nature of the breach and the context of the agreement.
perturbation.law_explanation: UCC § 2-302 allows a court to refuse to enforce a contract or clause it finds unconscionable at the time it was made. A one-day cure period for a material breach is unreasonably short and potentially unconscionable, particularly if the breach requires significant effort to remedy.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-302. Unconscionable contract or Clause. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-302. Unconscionable contract or Clause. (1) If the court as a matter of law finds the contract or any clause of the contract to have been unconscionable at the time it was made the court may refuse to enforce the contract, or it may enforce the remainder of the contract without the unconscionable clause, or it may so limit the application of any unconscionable clause as to avoid any unconscionable result. (2) When it is claimed or appears to the court that the contract or any clause thereof may be unconscionable the parties shall be afforded a reasonable opportunity to present evidence as to its commercial setting, purpose and effect to aid the court in making the determination. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The change from a five-day cure period to a one-day cure period for a material breach potentially contradicts the principle of unconscionability as described in UCC § 2-302. The Law Explanation suggests that a one-day period might be unreasonably short, especially if the breach requires significant effort to fix. Scraped Law Snippet 1, quoting UCC § 2-302, allows a court to refuse to enforce a contract or clause found to be unconscionable at the time it was made. The Perturbation Explanation supports this by stating that such a short cure period may violate the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing. This falls under 'Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction', where time-sensitive obligations do not align with legal requirements, potentially making the contractual terms unenforceable.
